unitTitle: GCSE Programming part 1 — sequence (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Translators — how computers understand our code
lessons.objectives:
I can explain that computers need exact, precise instructions
I can tell the difference between high-level and low-level languages
I can say why a translator is needed
I can describe the difference between a compiler and an interpreter
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: translator · compiler · interpreter · machine code · high-level · low-level. TA: hand out the starter; one counter per pair for the path activity. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — humans or computers? Pupils DRAG cards into Humans / Computers on the starter worksheet (card-sort, no writing). Think–pair–share.
   • Support: tick which one only does exactly what it is told.  • Core: write one thing a human can do that a computer cannot.  • Challenge: explain what the off-path sprite tells us. Likely error: “computers are cleverer” → fix-words “a computer only does EXACTLY what it is told, it cannot guess.”
2. (5–12) I-do — humans vs computers path. Same instructions to a person and a Scratch sprite; the sprite walks off the path because it cannot fill in the missing detail.
3. (12–32) We-do — lost in translation. Journey from machine code → assembly → high-level languages (Grace Hopper, Fortran). Pupils ORDER the journey on the activity worksheet (order block, drag).
4. (32–50) You-do — programming languages worksheet. Match the words (Support), fill the CPU gap + say why translators are needed (Core), compare compiler vs interpreter using the cake-recipe analogy (Challenge). TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; quick true/false recall of key terms. Keep a screenshot/photo of the mind map.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Sequence — your first Python code
lessons.objectives:
I can name the tools an IDE gives a programmer
I can run a Python program made of subroutines in sequence
I can predict what a sequence of code will do
I can spot and name syntax and logic errors
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: IDE · subroutine · sequence · syntax error · logic error. TA: everyone has the Python IDE (Mu or Trinket) open. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–3) Starter — why do we need translators? Recap from last lesson; fill-in-the-gap on the starter worksheet (machine code).
2. (3–8) I-do — what is an IDE? Colour coding, syntax highlighting, auto-indent, autocomplete, error checking, run in real time.
3. (8–22) I-do live coding — the welcome() program (a subroutine). Make a syntax error on purpose (delete a colon) then a logic error (do not call welcome()). Optional teacher-played live-coding video.
4. (22–37) We-do PRIMM — predict the Twinkle program. Only twinkle() is called, so only its lines run.
   • Support: card-sort syntax vs logic errors.  • Core: what is shown / why the rhyme stops.  • Challenge: Parsons — order the code to sing the whole verse. Likely error: forgetting to call a subroutine → “a subroutine only runs when it is CALLED.”
5. (37–52) You-do — run, investigate and modify on the activity worksheet; paste a Trinket link + screenshot.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; syntax vs logic error. TA notes who met all four.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: first Python code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Variables — named boxes that hold a value
lessons.objectives:
I can use meaningful names for variables
I can say why we need variables
I can explain assignment (giving a variable a value)
I can print a variable with print()
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: variable · assignment · initialisation · declaration · naming convention. TA: the silly-sentences start file is open for each pair. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — which file name is better? Pupils CHOOSE the clearest name (multiple choice, no writing) and say why.
2. (5–13) I-do — a variable is a named box that holds ONE value at a time. noun = "Car" puts "Car" in the box.
3. (13–20) We-do PRIMM — predict the silly sentences program; the same boxes are filled twice. Misconception fix: it only holds the LAST value put in it.
4. (20–48) You-do — investigate and make on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: card-sort good vs bad variable names.  • Core: match declaration/initialisation/assignment + fill the gap.  • Challenge: why it does not print “Car Zebra”; what the f-string does. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; peer-review a partner's silly story (variety of variables? any errors?).
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: silly sentences code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Input — adding interactivity and data types
lessons.objectives:
I can use input() to get an answer from the user
I can name the five data types (integer, real, Boolean, character, string)
I can use int() or float() to change a data type (casting)
I can say what a runtime error is
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: input · integer · real · Boolean · character · string · casting · runtime error. TA: the silly stories from last lesson are open; the data-collection start file is ready. Read the four “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — variables recap. Three multiple-choice questions (print syntax, set-before-use, one value at a time) on the starter worksheet.
2. (8–18) I-do live coding — input() waits for the user and stores what they type. Ask: “how could inputs improve our silly stories?” Optional teacher-played live-coding video.
3. (18–28) We-do — the five data types and casting. input() ALWAYS gives a string; int()/float() cast it to a number.
4. (28–48) You-do PRIMM — the mini data collection program on the activity worksheet.
   • Support: card-sort the values into data types.  • Core: what type input() gives + fill the int() gap.  • Challenge: the runtime error when “nine” is typed for age; what a validation check does. Likely error: typing letters for a number → a runtime error (ValueError) → “wrap input() in int() and check it.”
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the four “I can…”; recall the five data types. TA notes who met all four.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.

resources.title: data collection code (Teach Computing).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Flowcharts — plan a program with shapes
lessons.objectives:
I can name the main flowchart symbols
I can follow a flowchart and write the code it shows
I can design a simple flowchart for a program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: flowchart · terminator · subroutine · input · output. TA: Flowgorithm is open on each machine; pairs are set for pair programming. Read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — what will this flowchart do? Pupils PREDICT the output of the Lesson 2 flowchart (unfamiliar shapes) on the starter worksheet.
2. (5–10) I-do — the flowchart symbols: terminator (start/end), input/output (parallelogram), process (rectangle), subroutine (rectangle with side bars). Optional teacher-played Flowgorithm demo video.
3. (10–28) We-do/you-do pair programming — translate the “Susan from Space” chatbot flowchart into Python. Swap driver/navigator every 5 minutes; common-errors list on the worksheet. Likely error: missing int() around a number input.
4. (28–50) You-do — design a flowchart for the “always 3” mind-reading trick.
   • Support: match each symbol to its use.  • Core: order the trick steps.  • Challenge: code the first chatbot steps. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; say what three symbols are used for. TA notes who met all three.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound.
